title: State-level variation and multi-year trends in frequent mental distress among U.S. adults
text:
Show code
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(DT)
library(plotly)
library(scales)
library(htmltools)
Show code
brfss <- readRDS("brfss_subset.rds")

base_all <- brfss |> filter(!is.na(fmd))
base     <- base_all |> filter(year == 2024)

n_total_all <- nrow(brfss)
n_base_all  <- nrow(base_all)
n_base      <- nrow(base)
n_states    <- dplyr::n_distinct(base$state)
years_range <- range(base_all$year)

fmd_pal   <- c("FMD" = "#c0392b", "Not FMD" = "#7f8c8d")
fmd_color <- "#c0392b"

title: State-level variation and multi-year trends in frequent mental distress among U.S. adults
text:
Overall patterns and multi-year trends


Show code
f1_data <- base |>
  summarise(
    FMD     = mean(fmd),
    `Not FMD` = 1 - FMD,
    n       = n()
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(c(FMD, `Not FMD`), names_to = "Status", values_to = "Proportion") |>
  mutate(Status = factor(Status, levels = c("Not FMD", "FMD")),
         pct = Proportion * 100)

fmd_overall_pct <- f1_data$pct[f1_data$Status == "FMD"]

plot_ly(f1_data, x = ~Status, y = ~Proportion, type = "bar",
        marker = list(color = c("#7f8c8d", fmd_color)),
        text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", pct), textposition = "outside",
        hovertemplate = paste("%{x}: %{y:.1%}<br>N = ", comma(f1_data$n[1]),
                              "<extra></extra>")) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 1: Overall prevalence of Frequent Mental Distress (FMD)",
                               "<br><sub>U.S. adults, BRFSS 2024 (n = ",
                               comma(f1_data$n[1]), ")</sub>"), x = 0),
    yaxis = list(title = "Proportion", tickformat = ".0%",
                 range = c(0, max(f1_data$Proportion) * 1.15)),
    xaxis = list(title = ""),
    showlegend = FALSE, margin = list(t = 80)
  )






Figure 1 presents the overall prevalence of frequent mental distress (FMD) in the 2024 BRFSS analytic sample and serves as the starting point for the rest of the report. We include this figure first to introduce the project's main outcome and to establish a national-level reference point before examining differences across states, years, and subgroups.
Among 442,292 U.S. adults in the base cohort, 13.9% met the definition of FMD, meaning they reported poor mental health on 14 or more days in the past 30 days, while 86.1% did not. This indicates that frequent mental distress affects a substantial share of adults rather than a small exceptional group. Because the burden is already sizeable at the national level, the next step is to examine whether it is distributed evenly across states and populations, or whether certain places and groups bear a disproportionate share of that burden.


Show code
f2_year <- base_all |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")

plot_ly(f2_year, x = ~year, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "scatter", mode = "lines+markers+text",
        line = list(color = "#c0392b", width = 3),
        marker = list(color = "#c0392b", size = 10),
        text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct),
        textposition = "top center",
        hovertemplate = "Year %{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.2f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
        customdata = ~n) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 2: Overall FMD prevalence by BRFSS cycle, 2020–2024",
                               "<br><sub>Pooled U.S. adults, n = ",
                               comma(n_base_all), "</sub>"), x = 0),
    xaxis = list(title = "BRFSS cycle", dtick = 1),
    yaxis = list(title = "FMD prevalence (%)", ticksuffix = "%",
                 range = c(floor(min(f2_year$fmd_pct) - 1),
                           ceiling(max(f2_year$fmd_pct) + 1))),
    margin = list(t = 80)
  )








Show code
yr_lo <- f2_year$fmd_pct[f2_year$year == 2020]
yr_hi <- max(f2_year$fmd_pct)
yr_hi_year <- f2_year$year[which.max(f2_year$fmd_pct)]
yr_24 <- f2_year$fmd_pct[f2_year$year == 2024]


Figure 2 presents the national time trend in frequent mental distress (FMD) across the 2020–2024 BRFSS cycles. This figure is included because the project is concerned not only with geographic differences, but also with whether the burden of poor mental health has changed over time at the national level.
The pattern suggests a modest but overall upward trend. FMD prevalence increased from 12.3% in 2020 to 13.9% in 2024, with most of the rise occurring between 2020 and 2022. While the estimate dipped slightly in 2023, it did not return to earlier levels, and the highest prevalence was observed in 2024. Thus, the five-year pattern is more consistent with a gradual increase than with stability.
This national trend provides the broader context for the analyses that follow. If FMD has increased over time overall, the next question is whether that increase was shared similarly across states and demographic groups, or whether some populations experienced a greater increase than others.


Show code
f3_data <- base |>
  filter(!is.na(mental_days)) |>
  count(mental_days) |>
  mutate(
    highlight = case_when(
      mental_days == 0  ~ "0 days",
      mental_days == 30 ~ "30 days",
      mental_days >= 14 ~ "14+ days (FMD)",
      TRUE              ~ "1-13 days"
    ),
    highlight = factor(highlight,
                       levels = c("0 days", "1-13 days",
                                  "14+ days (FMD)", "30 days"))
  )

pal_hist <- c("0 days" = "#27ae60", "1-13 days" = "#bdc3c7",
              "14+ days (FMD)" = "#e67e22", "30 days" = "#c0392b")

plot_ly(f3_data, x = ~mental_days, y = ~n, color = ~highlight,
        colors = pal_hist, type = "bar",
        hovertemplate = "%{x} days: %{y:,} respondents<extra></extra>") |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = "Figure 3: Distribution of days of poor mental health (past 30), BRFSS 2024",
                 x = 0),
    xaxis = list(title = "Number of poor-mental-health days",
                 dtick = 5),
    yaxis = list(title = "Respondents"),
    legend = list(title = list(text = "Category"), orientation = "h",
                  x = 0.5, xanchor = "center", y = -0.2),
    barmode = "stack", margin = list(t = 60, b = 80)
  )






Figure 3 examines the distribution of MENTHLTH, the reported number of poor-mental-health days in the past 30 days. We include this figure to move beyond overall prevalence and better understand the shape of the underlying measure that defines the project's main outcome.
The distribution is strongly right-skewed and concentrated at key points rather than spread smoothly across the full range. Most respondents reported 0 days, while a smaller but nontrivial group reported very high values, especially 30 days. The middle range is comparatively sparse. This pattern suggests that persistent poor mental health is meaningfully different from occasional poor mental health, supporting the decision to use FMD (14 or more days) as the primary outcome in the later analyses.

title: State-level variation and multi-year trends in frequent mental distress among U.S. adults
text:
State-level variation
State-level prevalence of FMD in 2024 ranges widely, and the state × year heatmap in Figure 4c shows that the ordering is largely stable across cycles. The figures below show the same quantity in three complementary ways: a ranked bar chart for 2024, a choropleth map of 2024, and a state × year heatmap for 2020–2024.


Show code
state_tbl <- base |>
  group_by(state, abb) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(fmd_pct)

plot_ly(state_tbl,
        x = ~fmd_pct, y = ~reorder(state, fmd_pct), type = "bar",
        orientation = "h",
        marker = list(color = ~fmd_pct,
                      colorscale = list(c(0, "#3498db"),
                                        c(1, "#c0392b")),
                      showscale = FALSE),
        hovertemplate = paste("<b>%{y}</b><br>FMD: %{x:.1f}%<br>",
                              "n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>"),
        customdata = ~n) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 4a: Frequent Mental Distress prevalence by state, BRFSS 2024",
                               "<br><sub>50 jurisdictions, n = ",
                               comma(n_base), "</sub>"), x = 0),
    xaxis = list(title = "FMD prevalence (%)", ticksuffix = "%"),
    yaxis = list(title = "", automargin = TRUE),
    margin = list(l = 120, t = 70)
  )






Figure 4a shows the 2024 prevalence of frequent mental distress (FMD) across states, ordered from highest to lowest. We include this ranked bar chart because it provides the clearest direct comparison of states and allows us to assess whether the burden of FMD is distributed evenly across the country.
The pattern suggests notable geographic variation. States such as Kentucky, Arkansas, and Nevada appear near the top of the ranking, while North Dakota, the District of Columbia, and Nebraska are among the lowest. Although many states cluster in the middle, the full range is wide enough to indicate that state context may matter for understanding the distribution of frequent mental distress.
This figure is central to the project's state-level focus. It shows that the overall national prevalence conceals substantial interstate differences, providing motivation for the map and multi-year heatmap that follow.


Show code
plot_ly(state_tbl, type = "choropleth",
        locations = ~abb, locationmode = "USA-states",
        z = ~fmd_pct,
        colorscale = list(c(0, "#3498db"),
                          c(0.5, "#f39c12"),
                          c(1, "#c0392b")),
        colorbar = list(title = "FMD %", ticksuffix = "%"),
        hovertemplate = paste("<b>%{text}</b><br>FMD: %{z:.1f}%<extra></extra>"),
        text = ~state) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = "Figure 4b: State-level FMD prevalence (choropleth), BRFSS 2024",
                 x = 0),
    geo = list(scope = "usa", projection = list(type = "albers usa"),
               showlakes = TRUE, lakecolor = "white"),
    margin = list(t = 60)
  )






Figure 4b shows the 2024 state-level prevalence of frequent mental distress (FMD) as a choropleth map. This figure complements the ranked bar chart in Figure 4a by emphasizing the geographic pattern of variation rather than the exact ordering of states.
The map suggests that higher FMD prevalence is concentrated in parts of the South and Appalachian region, while lower prevalence appears more common in parts of the Upper Midwest and Great Plains. Thus, the interstate differences shown earlier do not appear geographically random.
Tennessee is blank because no reportable 2024 estimate was available in the public BRFSS file. As noted in the BRFSS documentation, the state did not collect enough data in 2024 to meet the minimum requirements for inclusion. This missing state should therefore be interpreted as unavailable data rather than low prevalence.


Show code
state_year <- base_all |>
  group_by(year, state) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")

state_order <- state_year |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarise(mean_pct = mean(fmd_pct, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(mean_pct) |>
  pull(state)

state_year <- state_year |> mutate(state = factor(state, levels = state_order))

plot_ly(state_year,
        x = ~year, y = ~state, z = ~fmd_pct, type = "heatmap",
        colorscale = list(c(0, "#3498db"),
                          c(0.5, "#f39c12"),
                          c(1, "#c0392b")),
        colorbar = list(title = "FMD %", ticksuffix = "%"),
        hovertemplate = paste("<b>%{y}</b>, %{x}<br>FMD: %{z:.1f}%<br>",
                              "n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>"),
        customdata = ~n) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 4c: State-level FMD prevalence by year, BRFSS 2020–2024",
                               "<br><sub>Rows sorted by 5-year mean FMD (lowest at bottom)</sub>"),
                 x = 0),
    xaxis = list(title = "BRFSS cycle", dtick = 1, side = "top"),
    yaxis = list(title = "", automargin = TRUE),
    margin = list(l = 120, t = 90)
  )






Figure 4c extends the state-level analysis by displaying FMD prevalence for each state across BRFSS cycles from 2020 to 2024. We include this heatmap because it allows both cross-state and across-year comparison in a single display, making it easier to assess whether state differences persist over time.
Overall, the heatmap suggests that state differences are fairly persistent across cycles. States that are relatively high in one year often remain toward the higher end in other years, while lower-prevalence states tend to remain lower over time. This indicates that the state-level variation shown in Figures 4a and 4b is not simply a one-year anomaly.
Blank cells indicate that no reportable BRFSS estimate was available for that specific state-year combination; they do not represent zero prevalence. For example, Tennessee is missing in 2024 because it was not included in the 2024 public-use file after failing to meet minimum reporting requirements. Similarly, Kentucky and Pennsylvania are missing in 2023 because the 2023 BRFSS documentation states that those states were unable to collect enough data to meet the minimum requirements for inclusion in the public data set. Other blank cells should be interpreted in the same way: as unavailable estimates rather than substantive zero values.


Show code
state_tbl |>
  arrange(desc(fmd_pct)) |>
  mutate(
    Rank = row_number(),
    n = comma(n),
    `FMD (%)` = sprintf("%.1f", fmd_pct)
  ) |>
  select(Rank, State = state, n, `FMD (%)`) |>
  datatable(
    rownames = FALSE,
    caption = "Table 1: FMD prevalence by state, sorted highest to lowest (BRFSS 2024).",
    options = list(pageLength = 10, order = list(list(0, "asc")))
  )








Show code
hi <- state_tbl |> arrange(desc(fmd_pct)) |> head(3)
lo <- state_tbl |> arrange(fmd_pct)       |> head(3)
range_pp <- diff(range(state_tbl$fmd_pct))


Table 1 reports the exact 2024 state-level FMD prevalence estimates and sample sizes that underlie Figures 4a–4c. It is included to supplement the visualizations with precise numeric values.
The table shows substantial interstate variation, with FMD prevalence ranging from 9.9% in North Dakota to 17.6% in Oregon and Kentucky. Other high-prevalence states include Louisiana, Arkansas, and Oklahoma, whereas New Hampshire, the District of Columbia, and South Dakota are among the lowest. This roughly 7.7-percentage-point spread reinforces the conclusion that the burden of frequent mental distress is not evenly distributed across states.
Because the table includes exact values, it also helps verify the patterns suggested by the ranked bar chart, map, and heatmap. These estimates are crude, unweighted prevalences and should therefore be interpreted as descriptive rather than fully adjusted comparisons.

title: State-level variation and multi-year trends in frequent mental distress among U.S. adults
text:
Demographic trends across years


Show code
f5_sex <- base_all |>
  filter(!is.na(sex)) |>
  group_by(year, sex) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")

f5_age <- base_all |>
  filter(!is.na(age_group)) |>
  mutate(age_bucket = case_when(
    age_group %in% c("18-24","25-29") ~ "18-29",
    age_group %in% c("30-34","35-39","40-44") ~ "30-44",
    age_group %in% c("45-49","50-54","55-59") ~ "45-59",
    age_group %in% c("60-64","65-69") ~ "60-69",
    TRUE ~ "70+"
  ),
  age_bucket = factor(age_bucket,
                      levels = c("18-29","30-44","45-59","60-69","70+"))) |>
  group_by(year, age_bucket) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")

p_sex <- plot_ly(f5_sex, x = ~year, y = ~fmd_pct, color = ~sex,
                 colors = c("Female" = "#c0392b", "Male" = "#2980b9"),
                 type = "scatter", mode = "lines+markers",
                 hovertemplate = "%{fullData.name}, %{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>") |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "BRFSS cycle", dtick = 1),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

age_colors <- c("18-29" = "#c0392b", "30-44" = "#e67e22",
                "45-59" = "#f1c40f", "60-69" = "#27ae60",
                "70+"   = "#2980b9")

p_age <- plot_ly(f5_age, x = ~year, y = ~fmd_pct, color = ~age_bucket,
                 colors = age_colors,
                 type = "scatter", mode = "lines+markers",
                 hovertemplate = "%{fullData.name}, %{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>") |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "BRFSS cycle", dtick = 1),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

subplot(p_sex, p_age, nrows = 1, titleX = TRUE, titleY = TRUE,
        margin = 0.07) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 5: FMD trends 2020–2024 by sex and by age band",
                               "<br><sub>Pooled U.S. adults, n = ",
                               comma(n_base_all), "</sub>"),
                 x = 0),
    legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0.5, xanchor = "center", y = -0.15),
    margin = list(t = 80, b = 70)
  )






Figure 5 examines how FMD trends differed by sex and age from 2020 to 2024. This figure is included to show which demographic groups are driving the broader increase in frequent mental distress observed at the national level.
The sex panel indicates that both men and women experienced increasing FMD prevalence over time, but women remained consistently higher in every cycle. The age panel shows even stronger separation: adults aged 18–29 had the highest prevalence throughout the period and experienced the sharpest increase, whereas adults aged 70+ remained the lowest in every year. Thus, the overall rise in FMD appears to be driven disproportionately by younger adults, while sex differences remained persistent across time.

title: State-level variation and multi-year trends in frequent mental distress among U.S. adults
text:
Behavioral and psychosocial correlates






Module note



The next figure uses the optional Social Determinants / Health Equity module, which only a subset of jurisdictions fielded in each cycle: SDLONELY appears in 2023 and 2024 only, while EMTSUPRT and LSATISFY appear in 2022–2024. Figure 6 pools the available cycles, with 409,918 respondents for loneliness and 645,864 for emotional support.




Show code
mk_group_df <- function(df, var, label) {
  df |>
    filter(!is.na(.data[[var]])) |>
    group_by(level = .data[[var]]) |>
    summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(variable = label)
}

f6_data <- bind_rows(
  mk_group_df(base_all, "loneliness", "Loneliness"),
  mk_group_df(base_all, "support",    "Emotional support")
) |>
  mutate(level = factor(level,
                        levels = c("Always", "Usually", "Sometimes",
                                   "Rarely", "Never")))

p_lone <- plot_ly(
  f6_data |> filter(variable == "Loneliness"),
  x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
  marker = list(color = "#c0392b"),
  text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct), textposition = "outside",
  hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
  customdata = ~n, name = "Loneliness"
) |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "How often feel lonely"),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

p_supp <- plot_ly(
  f6_data |> filter(variable == "Emotional support"),
  x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
  marker = list(color = "#2980b9"),
  text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct), textposition = "outside",
  hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
  customdata = ~n, name = "Support"
) |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "How often get needed emotional support"),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

subplot(p_lone, p_supp, nrows = 1, titleX = TRUE, titleY = TRUE,
        margin = 0.08) |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = paste0("Figure 6: FMD prevalence by loneliness and emotional support",
                    "<br><sub>BRFSS optional module (pooled 2022–2024 where available), n = ",
                    comma(sum(!is.na(base_all$loneliness))), "</sub>"),
      x = 0),
    showlegend = FALSE, margin = list(t = 80)
  )






Figure 6 explores the relationship between FMD and two psychosocial factors: loneliness and needed emotional support. These variables are included because they help explain which social experiences are most strongly associated with frequent mental distress.
The loneliness gradient is especially striking. FMD prevalence falls from 56.7% among adults who always feel lonely to 5.4% among those who never do, with a clear stepwise decline across intermediate categories. Emotional support shows a similarly strong pattern: adults who rarely receive the support they need have much higher FMD prevalence than those who usually or always do. Together, these panels suggest that loneliness and limited emotional support are among the strongest correlates shown in the report.
Because these measures come from an optional module, they are available only for selected jurisdictions and years, so the figure should be interpreted as pooled evidence from available cycles rather than a fully national trend. These patterns are also correlational and should not be interpreted as causal.


Show code
f7_ex <- base_all |>
  filter(!is.na(exercise)) |>
  group_by(level = exercise) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")

f7_gh <- base_all |>
  filter(!is.na(gen_health)) |>
  group_by(level = gen_health) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")

p_ex <- plot_ly(f7_ex, x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
                marker = list(color = c("#27ae60", "#c0392b")),
                text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct),
                textposition = "outside",
                hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
                customdata = ~n) |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "Any physical activity (past 30 days)"),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

p_gh <- plot_ly(f7_gh, x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
                marker = list(color = c("#16a085","#27ae60","#f1c40f",
                                        "#e67e22","#c0392b")),
                text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct),
                textposition = "outside",
                hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
                customdata = ~n) |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "Self-rated general health"),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

subplot(p_ex, p_gh, nrows = 1, titleX = TRUE, titleY = TRUE,
        margin = 0.08, widths = c(0.35, 0.65)) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 7: FMD prevalence by exercise and general health",
                               "<br><sub>Pooled BRFSS 2020–2024, n = ",
                               comma(n_base_all), "</sub>"),
                 x = 0),
    showlegend = FALSE, margin = list(t = 80)
  )






Figure 7 examines FMD prevalence by recent physical activity and self-rated general health. These variables are included to show how FMD relates to broader behavioral and health-status measures.
Adults who reported any physical activity in the past 30 days had lower FMD prevalence (11.4%) than those who reported none (19.7%). The general-health panel shows an even stronger gradient: FMD prevalence rises steadily from 5.6% among adults in excellent health to 44.5% among those in poor health. Together, these patterns suggest that frequent mental distress is strongly linked to broader patterns of inactivity and poor overall health.
Because self-rated general health overlaps conceptually with mental health, the right panel should be interpreted as descriptive association rather than causal evidence. Still, the size and monotonicity of the gradient make it one of the clearest correlates in the report.

title: State-level variation and multi-year trends in frequent mental distress among U.S. adults
text:
Socioeconomic and demographic differences


Show code
pct_by <- function(df, var) {
  df |>
    filter(!is.na(.data[[var]])) |>
    group_by(level = .data[[var]]) |>
    summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")
}

f8_age    <- pct_by(base_all, "age_group")
f8_income <- pct_by(base_all, "income")
f8_edu    <- pct_by(base_all, "education")

p_age <- plot_ly(f8_age, x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
                 marker = list(color = "#8e44ad"),
                 text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct),
                 textposition = "outside",
                 hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
                 customdata = ~n, name = "Age") |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "Age band"),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

p_inc <- plot_ly(f8_income, x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
                 marker = list(color = "#2980b9"),
                 text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct),
                 textposition = "outside",
                 hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
                 customdata = ~n, name = "Income") |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "Income (5-level harmonized)"),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

p_edu <- plot_ly(f8_edu, x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
                 marker = list(color = "#16a085"),
                 text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct),
                 textposition = "outside",
                 hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
                 customdata = ~n, name = "Education") |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "Education", tickangle = -25),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

subplot(p_age, p_inc, p_edu, nrows = 3, titleX = TRUE, titleY = TRUE,
        margin = 0.06) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 8: FMD prevalence by age, income, and education",
                               "<br><sub>Pooled BRFSS 2020–2024, n = ",
                               comma(n_base_all), "</sub>"),
                 x = 0),
    showlegend = FALSE, height = 820, margin = list(t = 80, b = 40)
  )








Show code
sex_gap <- base_all |> filter(!is.na(sex)) |>
  group_by(sex) |> summarise(pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")
mc_gap <- base_all |> filter(!is.na(med_cost)) |>
  group_by(med_cost) |> summarise(pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")


Figure 8 examines FMD prevalence across age, income, and education, highlighting how the burden of frequent mental distress is distributed across major demographic and socioeconomic gradients.
The age pattern is strongly monotone: FMD prevalence is highest among younger adults and falls steadily with age, from 22.8% among those aged 18–24 to 6.2% among those aged 80+. The income gradient is similarly clear, with prevalence declining from 29.1% in the lowest income group to 9.8% in the highest. Education also shows important patterning, though less perfectly linearly: prevalence is relatively high among respondents with lower educational attainment and lowest among college graduates.
Overall, the figure shows that FMD is disproportionately concentrated among younger and more socioeconomically disadvantaged adults.


Show code
f9_emp <- base_all |>
  filter(!is.na(employment)) |>
  group_by(level = employment) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(fmd_pct)) |>
  mutate(level = factor(level, levels = level))

emp_pal <- c("Employed for wages" = "#27ae60",
             "Self-employed"      = "#16a085",
             "Retired"            = "#2980b9",
             "Student"            = "#8e44ad",
             "Homemaker"          = "#f39c12",
             "Out of work <1 yr"  = "#e67e22",
             "Out of work 1+ yr"  = "#d35400",
             "Unable to work"     = "#c0392b")

plot_ly(f9_emp,
        x = ~fmd_pct, y = ~level, type = "bar", orientation = "h",
        marker = list(color = emp_pal[as.character(f9_emp$level)]),
        text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct), textposition = "outside",
        hovertemplate = "%{y}<br>FMD: %{x:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
        customdata = ~n) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 9: FMD prevalence by employment status",
                               "<br><sub>Pooled BRFSS 2020–2024, n = ",
                               comma(sum(!is.na(base_all$employment))), "</sub>"),
                 x = 0),
    xaxis = list(title = "FMD prevalence (%)", ticksuffix = "%",
                 range = c(0, max(f9_emp$fmd_pct) * 1.15)),
    yaxis = list(title = "", categoryorder = "array",
                 categoryarray = rev(levels(f9_emp$level)),
                 automargin = TRUE),
    showlegend = FALSE, margin = list(l = 140, t = 80)
  )








Show code
emp_hi <- f9_emp |> slice(1)
emp_lo <- f9_emp |> arrange(fmd_pct) |> slice(1)


Figure 9 shows FMD prevalence by employment status, adding another important socioeconomic dimension to the results. Employment status is relevant because it captures differences in labor-force attachment, disability, and economic stability that may be closely tied to mental health.
The strongest burden appears among adults unable to work (37.7%), followed by those out of work for 1 year or more (27.2%) and out of work for less than 1 year (25.4%). By contrast, retired (7.9%), self-employed (10.2%), and employed for wages (12.7%) adults show much lower prevalence. These differences indicate that FMD is strongly stratified by employment status.
Because the figure is descriptive, the pattern should not be interpreted causally. Mental distress may both contribute to and result from weaker labor-force attachment.


Show code
f10_mar <- base_all |>
  filter(!is.na(marital)) |>
  group_by(level = marital) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(fmd_pct)) |>
  mutate(level = factor(level, levels = level))

mar_pal <- c("Separated"        = "#c0392b",
             "Divorced"         = "#e67e22",
             "Never married"    = "#f39c12",
             "Unmarried couple" = "#8e44ad",
             "Widowed"          = "#2980b9",
             "Married"          = "#27ae60")

plot_ly(f10_mar,
        x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
        marker = list(color = mar_pal[as.character(f10_mar$level)]),
        text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct), textposition = "outside",
        hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
        customdata = ~n) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 10: FMD prevalence by marital status",
                               "<br><sub>Pooled BRFSS 2020–2024, n = ",
                               comma(sum(!is.na(base_all$marital))), "</sub>"),
                 x = 0),
    xaxis = list(title = "Marital status", categoryorder = "array",
                 categoryarray = levels(f10_mar$level)),
    yaxis = list(title = "FMD prevalence (%)", ticksuffix = "%",
                 range = c(0, max(f10_mar$fmd_pct) * 1.15)),
    showlegend = FALSE, margin = list(t = 80)
  )






Figure 10 shows FMD prevalence by marital status, adding another social dimension to the analysis. This figure is included because relationship status may capture differences in social support, household context, and recent life stress.
The highest prevalence appears among adults who are separated (25.3%), followed by those who are never married, in an unmarried couple, or divorced. Married adults have the lowest prevalence (9.3%), while widowed adults fall closer to the lower end of the distribution. These differences suggest that FMD is patterned not only by income, education, and employment, but also by partnership status and social context.
Because these comparisons are descriptive, they should not be interpreted causally. Marital status is likely entangled with age, financial conditions, and other life circumstances that may also shape mental distress.

title: State-level variation and multi-year trends in frequent mental distress among U.S. adults
text:
Lifestyle factors


Show code
f11_smk <- base_all |>
  filter(!is.na(smoker)) |>
  group_by(level = smoker) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(level = factor(level, levels = c("Never smoker", "Former smoker",
                                          "Occasional smoker", "Daily smoker")))

smk_pal <- c("Never smoker"      = "#27ae60",
             "Former smoker"     = "#2980b9",
             "Occasional smoker" = "#e67e22",
             "Daily smoker"      = "#c0392b")

plot_ly(f11_smk,
        x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
        marker = list(color = smk_pal[as.character(f11_smk$level)]),
        text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct), textposition = "outside",
        hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
        customdata = ~n) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 11: FMD prevalence by smoking status",
                               "<br><sub>Pooled BRFSS 2020–2024, n = ",
                               comma(sum(!is.na(base_all$smoker))), "</sub>"),
                 x = 0),
    xaxis = list(title = "Smoking status"),
    yaxis = list(title = "FMD prevalence (%)", ticksuffix = "%",
                 range = c(0, max(f11_smk$fmd_pct) * 1.15)),
    showlegend = FALSE, margin = list(t = 80)
  )






Figure 11 shows FMD prevalence by smoking status, adding a lifestyle-related correlate to the analysis. The pattern is strongly ordered: prevalence is lowest among never smokers and highest among daily smokers, with former and occasional smokers falling in between.
In particular, daily smokers report FMD prevalence of 25.7%, compared with 11.0% among never smokers. This large gap suggests that smoking status is strongly associated with the burden of frequent mental distress. Because the figure is descriptive, however, it should not be interpreted causally; smoking and mental distress may reinforce one another and may also reflect shared upstream risk factors.


Show code
f12_drk <- base_all |>
  filter(!is.na(drink_any)) |>
  group_by(level = drink_any) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")

f12_year <- base_all |>
  filter(!is.na(drink_any)) |>
  group_by(year, level = drink_any) |>
  summarise(n = n(), fmd_pct = mean(fmd) * 100, .groups = "drop")

p_drk <- plot_ly(f12_drk, x = ~level, y = ~fmd_pct, type = "bar",
                 marker = list(color = c("#8e44ad", "#16a085")),
                 text = ~sprintf("%.1f%%", fmd_pct),
                 textposition = "outside",
                 hovertemplate = "%{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<br>n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>",
                 customdata = ~n) |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "Any alcohol in past 30 days"),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

p_drk_yr <- plot_ly(f12_year, x = ~year, y = ~fmd_pct, color = ~level,
                    colors = c("Yes" = "#8e44ad", "No" = "#16a085"),
                    type = "scatter", mode = "lines+markers",
                    hovertemplate = "%{fullData.name}, %{x}<br>FMD: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>") |>
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "BRFSS cycle", dtick = 1),
         yaxis = list(title = "FMD (%)", ticksuffix = "%"))

subplot(p_drk, p_drk_yr, nrows = 1, titleX = TRUE, titleY = TRUE,
        margin = 0.08, widths = c(0.4, 0.6)) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = paste0("Figure 12: FMD prevalence by any alcohol use (pooled and by year)",
                               "<br><sub>BRFSS 2020–2024, n = ",
                               comma(sum(!is.na(base_all$drink_any))), "</sub>"),
                 x = 0),
    legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0.5, xanchor = "center", y = -0.2),
    margin = list(t = 80, b = 60)
  )






Figure 12 shows FMD prevalence by any alcohol use in the past 30 days, both pooled across years and separately by cycle. This figure is included because alcohol use is a relevant lifestyle factor, but its association with mental distress is more difficult to interpret than simpler behavioral measures.
Across the pooled sample, adults reporting any alcohol use had slightly lower FMD prevalence (12.5%) than those reporting none (14.3%), and this pattern was consistent across all five BRFSS cycles. Still, this should not be read as evidence that drinking is protective. The "no alcohol" category likely includes people who abstain because of illness, medication, prior alcohol dependence, or other health-related reasons, while the variable itself does not distinguish moderate from heavy drinking. The figure should therefore be interpreted as a descriptive comparison of broad groups rather than as evidence of a beneficial effect of alcohol use.

title: Data Processing
text:
Show code
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(DT)
library(plotly)
library(scales)
Show code
brfss <- readRDS("brfss_subset.rds")

base_all <- brfss |> filter(!is.na(fmd))
base_2024 <- base_all |> filter(year == 2024)

n_total_all <- nrow(brfss)
n_base_all  <- nrow(base_all)
n_base_2024 <- nrow(base_2024)
n_states_2024 <- dplyr::n_distinct(base_2024$state)

title: Data Processing
text:
Records by year


Show code
year_counts <- brfss |>
  count(year, name = "n")

plot_ly(year_counts,
        x = ~year, y = ~n, type = "bar",
        marker = list(color = "#2980b9"),
        text = ~comma(n), textposition = "outside",
        hovertemplate = "Year %{x}<br>Rows: %{y:,}<extra></extra>") |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = "Exploratory check: analytic rows available by year", x = 0),
    xaxis = list(title = "BRFSS cycle", dtick = 1),
    yaxis = list(title = "Rows retained after cleaning"),
    showlegend = FALSE,
    margin = list(t = 60)
  )

title: Data Processing
Data Processing
section: Distribution of poor mental health days
text:
Distribution of poor mental health days


Show code
mental_days_dist <- base_2024 |>
  filter(!is.na(mental_days)) |>
  count(mental_days) |>
  mutate(
    group = case_when(
      mental_days == 0  ~ "0 days",
      mental_days == 30 ~ "30 days",
      mental_days >= 14 ~ "14+ days",
      TRUE              ~ "1-13 days"
    ),
    group = factor(group, levels = c("0 days", "1-13 days", "14+ days", "30 days"))
  )

plot_ly(
  mental_days_dist,
  x = ~mental_days, y = ~n, color = ~group,
  colors = c("0 days" = "#27ae60", "1-13 days" = "#bdc3c7",
             "14+ days" = "#e67e22", "30 days" = "#c0392b"),
  type = "bar",
  hovertemplate = "%{x} days<br>Respondents: %{y:,}<extra></extra>"
) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = "Exploratory check: poor mental health days in 2024", x = 0),
    xaxis = list(title = "Days of poor mental health in the last 30 days", dtick = 5),
    yaxis = list(title = "Respondents"),
    legend = list(title = list(text = "Category"), orientation = "h",
                  x = 0.5, xanchor = "center", y = -0.2),
    barmode = "stack",
    margin = list(t = 60, b = 80)
  )






This distribution is one reason the dichotomized FMD outcome is useful here: respondents cluster heavily at 0 days and again at the extreme upper tail, rather than forming a smooth continuous distribution.
